They reported that the larger microspheres fabricated with a low
(0.1%, w/v) PVA concentration in the external water phase have
a more rapid BSA release. The oil-phase volume and the poly-
mer concentration also play a critical role in the determination
of morphology and release proﬁles of microspheres [7]. A low
oil volume yields a matrix characterized by more numerous and
bigger pores with a fast BSA release. Microspheres fabricated
with lower polymer concentration have a more porous matrix
compared with microspheres prepared with higher concentra-
tion.

by the nanoparticles’ surface. Fig. 5B shows that microparticles
formulated by poloxamer possessed completely different surface
almost without pores, which might have substantial effect on the
protein release.

Highest BSA release was observed at microparticles
fab-
ricated by PVP (Fig. 4c). Their structure showed completely
different character from the particles discussed above (Fig. 5C).
Huge initial BSA release occurred. The burst phase is generally
considered to result from loss of surface protein [20], thus sig-
niﬁcant part (>50%) of BSA should have been located on the

surface of particles. Contrarily, Coombes et al.
[6]
found that
microparticles formed by PVP released less protein and slower
than those made by PVA. However, they applied extremely high
concentration of the emulsiﬁers (each 10%, w/v), which could
cause important variation of the particle structure. Taking into
account that in vivo degradation is probably faster than in vitro
